fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ANDERSON J:

INTRODUCTION

These Reasons for Judgment relate to parenting arrangements for the child of the parties’ relationship, namely, a female child who is now aged five (5) years.
The parties were engaged in a final hearing before me between 15 and 19 September 2025 and again on 25 September 2025, 26 November 2025 and 28 November 2025. The final hearing in addition to the parties’ parenting applications related to:
(a) An application made by the mother pursuant to s 106B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). This application related to efforts by the mother to set aside a transfer of the former matrimonial home to a person described variously as the father’s sister and/or wife;[1] and

(b) An application made by the mother pursuant to s 79 of the Act.

It is not necessary for me to address the applications identified above in detail given that with the consent of the parties and the third party referred to above, I made final orders under Part VIII of the Act on 26 November 2025. Some of the evidence, which I heard, however, does infect my consideration of the parties’ competing parenting applications and I will address that evidence where I consider it appropriate to do so. Further, it is against the background of bitter litigation with respect to financial issues that it falls to me to determine the parenting arrangements for the parties’ daughter.
For the reasons discussed below, I have determined that it is appropriate to make orders that the child live with the mother and spend daytime periods with the father.

FACTUAL BACKGROUND

The father is aged fifty-two (52) years. He was born in Country G.[2] He is now an Australian Citizen.[3] He works as a driver.[4]
The mother is aged thirty-eight (38) years.[5] She too was born in Country G and is a permanent resident in Australia.[6] She is a homemaker.[7]
The parties met in December 2015 and married in City F in 2016.[8] The marriage enabled the mother to obtain a spouse visa, and she migrated to Australia in early 2017.[9]
In December 2019 the parties returned to Country G. Thereafter, the mother commenced in vitro fertilisation treatment, and she fell pregnant in 2020. Although the father returned to Australia, the mother says that she was unable to do so given medical treatment, which she had received.[10]
The child was born in Country G in 2020 at the height of the Covid-19 pandemic.[11] As a consequence of the pandemic, the mother and the child could not return to Australia until July 2021.[12]
The mother says that the relationship between the parties deteriorated in December 2022 whilst the parties were again in Country G. Particularly, she says that the parties argued over the father’s desire for the mother to undergo in vitro fertilisation again so that the parties might have another child. The mother refused, and as a consequence, the mother says that the father returned to Australia without her.[13]
For his part, the father says that the relationship between the parties deteriorated after the mother had an affair with a man who by his affidavit described himself as the mother’s partner.[14] The parties agree that in March 2023, the mother and the child moved into a separate bedroom at the former matrimonial home.[15] Notwithstanding this, the father says that he still cared for the child.[16] He makes that assertion in circumstances where he otherwise deposes to working from 6.00am to 4.00pm five (5) days each week during the relationship.[17]
In March 2023, the mother returned to Country G to mark the occasion of her father’s death. She remained there until April 2023 having also obtained advice in Country G about filing for a divorce.[18] The mother says that the parties signed documents issued by the relevant authority in Country G in 2023.[19] This is denied by the father. He says that the mother forged his signature.[20]
The mother left the former matrimonial home with the child on 18 June 2023.[21]
A few days prior to the mother’s departure from the former matrimonial home, the father says that the parties had “discussions” after the mother “discovered” that she had been recorded by a Closed-Circuit Television system, which had been installed at the former matrimonial home.[22] The father says that the mother became angry and thereafter smashed the CCTV cameras with a hammer. Although the mother says that she became aware of the cameras in about March 2023, the effect of the mother’s evidence is that she initially tolerated it but that by June 2023, she became tired of the father’s controlling behaviour. This being so, the mother says that she removed the cameras only for the same to be reinstalled by the father. The mother then agrees that she damaged the cameras so that the father would “stop recording me, stalking me and using these [sic] video footage as intimidation”.[23]
The father reported the mother’s damage of the cameras to New South Wales Police, and the mother was arrested in June 2023.[24] The mother says that:
(a) She was then identified as a Defendant in an Apprehended Violence Order;[25]

(b) Police advised her to leave the property. She did so together with the child who at that stage was only two (2) years old. On the mother’s version of events, police did not allow her the opportunity to obtain basic necessities or clothes for herself and the child.[26] The mother says that the father then failed to respond to her requests to collect necessities for her and the child and that the father otherwise changed the locks to the parties’ property.[27] The mother says that she obtained assistance from a state court to enable her to return to the property in July 2023;[28]

(c) The father changed the locks to the property asserting that the mother’s partner is a criminal despite the fact (as discussed below) that the mother’s partner has no criminal history whatsoever; and

(d) Charges against the mother were withdrawn in March 2024.[29]

The father says that prior to June 2023, the family had no involvement with police. Similarly, he says that the family had no involvement with the Department of Communities and Justice.[30]
The father commenced proceedings in Division Two of this Court in July 2023.
In September 2023, and with the consent of the parties, a Senior Judicial Registrar made orders that the child spend time with the father as follows:
(a) Commencing on 30 September 2023 and continuing for a period of three (3) months on each Saturday from 10.00am to 4.30pm; and

(b) Thereafter, on each alternate weekend on both Saturday and Sunday from 10.00am to 4.30pm.

The same orders provided for the parties to have equal shared parental responsibility for the child. Again, those orders were made with the consent of each party.
The father did not take up the opportunity to spend time with the child on alternate Sundays as he was permitted to do by the consent orders made in September 2023 (supra). On cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer, the father said that he did not do so because the mother “did not let me”. He then said that in circumstances where he moved house subsequent to September 2023 and “had paper everywhere” he may have overlooked the additional time that was afforded to him by the September 2023 orders. Notwithstanding this, he had been communicating with the child on Tuesday and Thursday evenings by video call.[31]
The mother says as follows with respect to time spent between the child and her father:
(a) On 7 October 2023, and having spent the day with the father, she noticed a “long scratch” on the child’s buttocks. The child allegedly disclosed to her mother that she injured herself but that her father ignored the injury;[32]

(b) In November 2023, and in circumstances where the child was ill, the mother asked the father whether he would forego his time with the child pursuant to the September 2023 orders (supra). The father would not agree, and the mother says that the father then ignored her various efforts across the course of the day to enquire as to the welfare of the child.[33] The mother says that on the evening of [...] November 2023 she took the child to a medical practitioner because she had pain in her abdomen. I discuss the mother’s criticisms of the father’s behaviour on this occasion below;

(c) On 16 March 2024, the child returned to her care with a bruise in the area of the child’s genitalia. The child subsequently disclosed that she hurt herself on a slide whilst playing with the father but as discussed below, the mother was critical that the father did not bring the injury to her attention;[34] and

(d) In September 2024, the child refused to transition into the father’s care. The mother says that as a consequence, the father called her a “whore” and threatened to hit her. The mother then asked her partner to drive her to a police station where she made a report with respect to the father’s conduct. Initially, the mother did not consider that her concern was taken seriously so she attended at an alternate police station. As a consequence of those efforts, the father was identified as a defendant in an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order and charged with assault.[35] The charges were withdrawn in April 2025.[36] As I discuss below, it is the mother’s contention that the father fabricated evidence so as to achieve such an outcome.

The child has not spent time with the father since September 2024.[37] There is controversy about whether the father attended at the changeover location in the weeks subsequent to September 2024. However, the father conceded that he attended at the changeover location on 19 October 2024 at which time, the child again refused to transition into his care. Thereafter, and on the father’s oral evidence, he did not attend at the changeover location because he “did not want [the mother] to sue me for harassment”.
It is against this background that the parties’ competing parenting applications fall for determination.

EVIDENCE

The parties filed a plethora of applications, affidavits and financial statements, which were relevant to the Part VIII application and the mother’s application pursuant to s 106B of the Act.
With respect to the parties’ competing parenting applications, the documents filed by the father and relevant to the issues to be determined include:
(a) An Amended Initiating Application filed on 22 March 2024;

(b) An affidavit in the name of the father filed on 12 June 2025; and

(c) An Outline of Case Document dated 12 September 2025 but not formally filed.

The father did not invite me to mark any documents as Exhibits in the parenting proceedings.
The documents relied on by the mother, which are relevant to my determination of the parties’ competing financial applications include:
(a) A Third Amended Response to Initiating Application filed on 28 May 2025;

(b) An affidavit in the name of the mother filed on 28 May 2025;

(c) An affidavit in the name of the mother’s partner filed on 4 July 2025;

(d) An Outline of Case Document filed on 10 September 2025; and

(e) Some tendered documents.[38]

The Independent Children’s Lawyer relied on:
(a) A Family Report prepared by a Court Child Expert dated 12 June 2024;

(b) An updated Family Report prepared by a Court Child Expert dated 12 November 2025;

(c) An Outline of Case Document filed 11 September 2025; and

(d) Tendered documents.[39]

COMPETING PROPOSALS

The father by his Outline of Case Document promoted orders in terms, which were different to his Amended Initiating Application save that as his ultimate position, the father’s application was the same. That is, and after a period of gradually increasing time, the father sought orders that the child spend time with him on each alternate weekend from the conclusion of school on Friday to 5.00pm on Sunday.
By his Outline of Case Document, the father:
(a) Abandoned his application to spend half of all school holiday periods with the child; and

(b) Conceded that it is appropriate to make an order that the mother have sole decision-making responsibility for all major long-term issues affecting the child. The father made that concession on the basis that he otherwise sought an order that the mother be obliged to advise him in writing at least twenty-eight (28) days prior to making any major decision concerning the child’s health and education and that the mother otherwise be obliged to consult him. The mother did not promote an order that she be obliged to consult the father. That position was also adopted by the Independent Children’s Lawyer although she did promote an order that the mother advise the father in writing of “any significant decision she makes about the child’s major [sic] health, education and religion as soon as practicable after she has made this decision”.[40]

The mother’s application remained unchanged throughout the trial. Particularly, the mother promoted orders that the child live with her and spend no time with the father or otherwise communicate with him. The mother also sought orders for her protection and the protection of the child pursuant to s 68B of the Act and otherwise sought orders permitting her to travel with the child outside of the Commonwealth of Australia.
For her part, the Independent Children’s Lawyer also sought an order that the mother be afforded sole decision-making responsibility in relation to major long-term issues affecting the child. With respect to parenting arrangements, the Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted orders that:
(a) The child live with the mother;

(b) For a period of six (6) months, the child spend supervised time with her father at a children’s contact service;

(c) Thereafter, and for a period of three (3) months, the child spend time with the father each Saturday from 10.00am to 1.00pm; and

(d) Thereafter, the child spend time with the father on each Saturday from 10.00am to 4.00pm with such time to be suspended on the first two (2) Saturdays of each of the Term One, Term Two and Term Three school holiday periods.[41]

The Independent Children’s Lawyer otherwise promoted time between the child and her father on special occasions and promoted a range of ancillary orders.

PARENTING PROCEEDINGS – LEGAL PRINCIPLES TO BE APPLIED AND PROCEDURE TO BE FOLLOWED

Given the gravity of the matters alleged by the mother, it is useful to set out s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth):
Civil proceedings: standard of proof
(1) In a civil proceeding, the court must find the case of a party proved if it is satisfied that the case has been proved on the balance of probabilities.

(2) Without limiting the matters that the court may take into account in deciding whether it is so satisfied, it is to take into account:

(a) the nature of the cause of action or defence; and

(b) the nature of the subject-matter of the proceeding; and

(c) the gravity of the matters alleged.
Section 140(2) is a codification of the oft-quoted remarks of Dixon J in the High Court decision of Briginshaw v Briginshaw (1938) 70 CLR 336. His Honour said this at p.362:
The seriousness of an allegation made, the inherent unlikelihood of an occurrence of a given description, or the gravity of the consequences flowing from a particular finding are considerations which much effect the answer to the question whether the issue has been proved to the reasonable satisfaction of the tribunal. In such matters “reasonable satisfaction” should not be produced by inexact proofs, in definite testimony, or indirect process.
It is well settled that it is not necessary for a trial judge, in reaching a decision, to refer to every piece of evidence or argument presented during the trial. In Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon,[42] Gleeson CJ, McHugh and Gummow JJ said this:
...A judge's reasons are not required to mention every fact or argument relied on by the losing party as relevant to an issue. Judgments of trial judges would soon become longer than they already are if a judge's failure to mention such facts and arguments would be evidence that he or she had not properly considered the losing party's case.
In Housing Commission of New South Wales v Tatmar Pastoral Co Pty Ltd and Penrith Pastoral Co Pty Ltd,[43] Mahoney JA said this:
It is not the duty of the judge to decide every matter which is raised in argument.

...

Nor is it necessary for a judge who is exercising a discretionary judgment to detail each factor which he has found to be relevant or irrelevant, or to itemize, for example, in the assessment of damages for tort, each of the factual matters to which he has had regard ... Nor is a judge required to make an explicit finding on each disputed piece of evidence. It will be sufficient, if the inference as to what is found is appropriately clear...

Best Interests of the child

When making parenting orders, the Court is mandated to regard the child’s best interests as the paramount consideration (ss 60CA and 65AA of the Act). The Act specifies the criteria which must be considered in arriving at a conclusion as to what is in the child’s best interests (s 60CC). The six (6) considerations referred to in s 60CC(2) are non-hierarchical. The Court is obliged to consider:
(a) arrangements that promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child;

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or will have parental responsibility to provide for the child’s needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so; and

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

When considering the arrangements, which would promote the safety of the child and each person who has the care of the child, the Court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child, and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.
Section 60CG of the Act imposes a statutory imperative to ensure that a parenting order does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence and empowers the Court to include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
Family violence is defined in s 4AB of the Act and means violent, threatening, or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member) or causes the family member to be fearful. Examples of such behaviour include assault, sexually abusive behaviour, stalking, repeated derogatory taunts, intentional damage or destruction of property, unreasonably withholding financial support needed to meet reasonable living expenses of the family member or his or her child, at a time when the family member is entirely or predominantly dependent on the person for financial support, preventing the family member from making or keeping connection with his or her family or friends, or unlawfully depriving the family member from his or her liberty.
Section 4AB(3) and (4) of the Act makes it clear that children are to be regarded as exposed to family violence either through direct sensory perception of violent action or such perception of consequences of violent action.
As the facts referred to above make plain, this is a case about risk and the arrangements, which might promote the safety of the child.
Where ultimately the Court’s focus is on formulating orders which attend to the child’s best interests, the Court is required to make findings of fact. The standard of proof is one of “on the balance of probabilities” consistent with s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (supra).
A party making an assertion of fact has an onus to prove that fact on the balance of probabilities although the task for the Court ultimately remains as one of weighing and balancing the probity of evidence towards a determination of the child’s best interests.
The mother in this case invites me to make a finding that the father presents an unacceptable risk of harm to the child on account of child’s “exposure to domestic violence, physical and emotional harm and potential neglect”.[44]
When considering the submissions of the parties, I am mindful that the test for determining unacceptable risk was laid down by the High Court in M & M (1998) 166 CLR 69 (“M & M”). Whilst the risk with which the Court was concerned in that case was the risk of sexual abuse, the principles are applicable – and have been applied – to the assessment of risk more generally.
If a finding is made that a child is at a risk of harm in the care of a parent, consideration must be given to the orders, if any, that may be made to mitigate or ameliorate those risks so as to enable the child to have a relationship with the parent (see B & B [1993] FamCA 143; (1993) FLC 92-357).
The Full Court’s decision in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 (“Isles”), summarised the authorities on unacceptable risk outlined in M v M. The Court in Isles also referred to the decision in N and S and the Separate Representative (1996) FLC 92-655 as follows:
Fogarty J stated it is necessary for a trial judge to give real and substantial consideration to the facts of the case and to decide whether or not, and why or why not, those facts could be said to raise an unacceptable risk of harm to the child and, furthermore, the qualitative analysis of the evidence must be directed not just to the existence of the risk of harm but also the magnitude of the possible harm...
(citations omitted)
In Isles, the Court at [35] went on to quote Tree J in Bant v Clayton [2015] FamCAFC 222; (2015) 53 Fam LR 621:
Risk assessment involves determining firstly, the degree of the likelihood of the postulated event, and secondly, the prospect and magnitude of harm that may flow if the event occurs. The weighing of those two considerations – even accepting they may be imprecisely expressed within parameters – will inform whether the risk is adjudged to be acceptable or not. However that conclusion cannot be made in the abstract.
The Court agreed with and adopted Austin J’s judgment in Fitzwater & Fitzwater [2019] FamCAFC 251; (2019) 60 Fam LR 212 as being the correct statement of the law. Justice Austin’s judgment includes the following:
The assessment of risk is a predictive exercise and while it is, naturally enough, liable to be influenced by factual findings about past events, the contemplation of risk entails the foresight of possible harm. It is an oddity to expect that the mere possibility of future harm can or should be proven as a probability, as has been implied before (Potter and Potter [2007] FamCA 350; (2007) FLC 93-326 at [110], [129]). Risks of harm must be heeded even if they are improbable eventualities.
Speaking of the risk of some future occurrence is just another way of expressing the chance of it happening. The concept of chance lies along a continuum, encompassing all outcomes which lie in the range between highly probable and remotely possible, assuming the polar extremes of certainty are ignored. In the current context, the higher the chance of the children’s sexual abuse, the greater the risk of their physical or psychological harm. At some point on the continuum the risk of such harm becomes so potent it cannot be tolerated: it is unacceptable.
In Hickson & Matthew [2022] FedCFamC1A 161, Deputy Chief Justice McClelland said this of Justice Austin’s analysis at [39]:
Additionally and relevantly for the purpose of this appeal, I would expand upon that insightful analysis by Austin J with the following guidance that emerges from authorities:
(1) It is now well established that “unacceptable risk” includes not merely physical harm but also includes an assessment of the risk of emotional harm.

(2) Such an unacceptable risk can include any or all matters that compromise the safety, welfare and well-being of a child, and is examined in light of an accumulation of factors proved.

(3) The components which lead to a conclusion that an unacceptable risk exists need not each be established on the balance of probabilities. The Court may reach a conclusion of “unacceptable risk” from the accumulation of factors, none or only some of which are proved to that standard, endorsing and applying the principles set out in a paper prepared by the Hon John Fogarty AM, “Unacceptable Risk: A Return to Basics” (2006) 20 Australian Journal of Family Law 249.

(4) While each factor establishing risk need not be proved to the standard of s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), insofar as determining whether an unacceptable risk exists involves a prediction of the future, based on findings of fact: “the confidence one will have in the prediction will be, in part, a reflection of the confidence one has in the factual findings that base the prediction”.

(citations omitted)
I have read the material filed by the parties and listened to their submissions. I am not however required to address every fact or submission made.[45] Nor have I done so. However, every piece of evidence relied upon by the parties has been read and carefully considered by me.

ISSUES TO BE DETERMINED

The father makes allegations of family violence against the mother. Particularly, the father says that the mother verbally abused and intimidated the child and that the mother’s behaviour was captured by the household CCTV cameras.[46] He also says that whilst intoxicated, the mother hit the child with her own head and caused bruising.[47] With respect to the mother’s partner, the father asserted that he “has had involvement with drug cultivation in the past and is well known in [Suburb E]”.[48] The mother denied the father’s allegations to the effect that she was physically violent to the child.[49] In any event, the father’s evidence on this topic was inconsistent conceding under cross-examination by the mother’s counsel that “to our daughter she didn’t do anything wrong”.
It is not necessary for me to determine the accuracy (or otherwise) of the allegations made by the father as I do not consider that my efforts would authentically influence the determination of the dispute.[50] Particularly, I am mindful of the decision of the Full Court in Pickford & Pickford wherein at [87], Justices Austin and Williams said as follows:
Even if the parties did inadvisedly challenge one another about disputed allegations of historical family violence, either in cross-examination or by duelling submissions, given how the disputed allegations did not seem genuinely material to the outcome of the narrow parenting issue left to decide, one wonders why it was necessary for his Honour to appease the parties by engaging with their debate. It is apposite to observe how judges need not make findings to resolve contested facts unless the findings authentically influence the outcome. In fact, the High Court of Australia has expressly cautioned against unnecessary factual findings in the context of risk assessment (M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69 at 76–77) (“M v M”). So has this Court (Eastley & Eastley [2022] FedCFamC1A 101; (2022) FLC 94-094 at [18] and [31]) (“Eastley”).
When arriving at this conclusion, I bring to account the following:
(a) By his proposed order, the father consents to an outcome that the mother remain the child’s primary carer;

(b) The father did not seek any injunctive order in relation to the mother’s use of alcohol;

(c) The father did not seek any order for the mother to submit to a test, which may have given an indication as to the extent of her alcohol use; and

(d) The father did not seek any injunctive order in relation to the mother’s partner. In any event, the mother’s partner has no criminal history whatsoever.[51]

For these reasons, it must necessarily be the father’s position that the child is safe in the care of the mother.
For her part, the mother says that:
(a) The father was controlling such that he “controlled my every movement and every dollar I spent”;[52]

(b) The father perpetrated family violence both during and subsequent to the relationship;

(c) The father lacks the parenting capacity and emotional regulation necessary to parent the child; and

(d) Her mental health will be impacted if she is required by an Order of this Court to facilitate time between the child and the father.

The mother’s overall position as expressed by her counsel’s Case Outline Document remains as follows:
The mother says the father poses an unacceptable risk of harm to the child through to [sic] exposure to domestic violence, physical and emotional harm and potential neglect.

The impact on the mother to facilitate contact with the father will potentially impact her mental health and jeopardise her ability to provide care as the primary caregiver to the child.[53]
Against this background, I consider that I am obliged to determine the following issues:
(a) Whether the father engaged in family violence as defined in s 4AB of the Act and if so, does the father present an unacceptable risk of harm to the child?

(b) Does the father present risk of harm of to the child on the basis that he lacks parenting capacity?

(c) Whether the mother’s parenting capacity would be adversely impacted if the Court makes an order for time spending between the child and her father; and

(d) The potential adverse impact upon the child of a permanent separation from her father.

Has the father engaged in family violence as defined in s 4AB of the Act and if so, does the father present an unacceptable risk of harm to the child?

The mother’s narrative of her life with the father in the early years of the parties’ relationship is one of joint endeavour. She deposes to waking up at 3.00am or 4.00pm to assist the father to get ready for work. She says that if she did not prepare the father’s breakfast or lunch the father would “get mad and yell at me”.[54] The father was not cross-examined about that allegation, and the evidence is not sufficient to permit me to make a finding. She otherwise says that she assisted the father with his driving business. It is in the context of this joint endeavour that the mother makes allegations against the father.

Events of June 2018

The mother says that in June 2018, and whilst pregnant with twins, she began to experience severe pain such that she was required to lay on the floor. She says that despite her requests, the father declined to return from work to assist her. The mother suffered from internal bleeding and subsequently lost the children. On arrival at hospital, the mother says that she was advised that her life was at risk on account of the delay occasioned by the father.[55] She did not produce any evidence to support that assertion.
For his part, the father denies that he deliberately delayed returning home to the mother so as to cause her injury. He says that in the course of remote work, he sometimes has mobile phone reception and sometimes does not. He then says:
When I finally stopped [work] I checked the phone, [redacted] drove back home and took her immediately to the hospital. The nurses couldn’t see her when she arrived and she had a 3 hour wait.[56]
On cross-examination by the father’s counsel, the mother conceded that she could have used a telephone to contact emergency services but denied that she could have properly explained her symptoms given her inability to speak English fluently. I accept that the mother may have been unable to explain her symptoms, but I find it difficult to understand why she did not contact emergency services in any event.
The mother’s miscarriage was not a topic, which was canvassed by the Court Child Expert. Further, the written and oral evidence of the parties does not enable me to make a finding that the father deliberately delayed taking the mother to hospital so that she might receive treatment. Such behaviour in any event would be inconsistent with:
(a) Efforts by the parties to conceive a child by means of in vitro fertilisation;

(b) The mother’s evidence that the father expressed a desire to have a second child;

(c) The father’s involvement in this litigation for twenty-eight months (by the time that the trial concluded) so that he might spend time with his daughter; and

(d) The content of a Statutory Declaration made by the father in support of the mother’s application for a partner visa in February 2018.[57] That document was admitted into evidence only as to paragraphs 4 and 8. At paragraph 8, the father deposed to his attendance on the mother on each day she was in hospital following her miscarriage. That assertion is inconsistent with a finding that the father deliberately sought to cause the mother to have a miscarriage.

For these reasons, I do not find that the father’s behaviour in June 2018 constituted an act of family violence. Even if such a conclusion is erroneous, the mother herself did not consider that the father’s actions that day precluded the making of an order to the effect that the child spend unsupervised time with the father. Indeed, and when the mother filed a Response to Initiating Application on 15 September 2023,[58] the mother promoted interim and final orders for unsupervised time. I discuss this topic in further detail below.

Events of December 2021

The mother says that in December 2021, she found herself in excruciating pain. She says that the father ignored her plight and advised her to take a pain killer. The mother says at 11.00pm, she went to the toilet and in the course of doing so, fell to the ground losing consciousness. Again, the mother says that the father did not take her to hospital until she begged him to do so and again, the mother says that absent such intervention, it is likely that she would have died.[59] The allegation is made in circumstances where the maternal grandmother was otherwise visiting the family from Country G.
The allegation is also made in circumstances where the father says that he agreed to take the mother to hospital as soon as the mother requested him to do so. Otherwise, the father says that the mother had by telephone consulted a friend in Country G for advice.[60]
The mother’s partner describes the father as being “insufficiently attentive” to the mother on the occasions on which the mother was hospitalised (supra).[61]
It may be that the father was insufficiently attentive to the mother’s plight. However, the evidence as presented on this topic is not sufficient for me to find that the father’s behaviour constitutes family violence as defined by s 4AB(1) of the Act.

Events of August 2022

The mother says that in August 2022, she was cooking for the family whilst the maternal grandmother, the father and the child were in the living room. She alleges that the child began to cry and in response, the father “used an arm and lifted [the child] by the back of her shirt and put her into the room opposite the toilet and locked her up in there”. The mother says that this action caused the child to cry “even more because she was scared of the dark room and being locked up all by herself”.[62] The father denies the allegation and says that he took his daughter to the bedroom so that he might calm her after she suffered a minor injury.[63]
On cross-examination, the father said that he picked the child up by her waist when she was crying and carried her to a bedroom. He said that he stayed in the bedroom with the child so that he might soothe her. He conceded that he did not wish for the maternal grandmother to interfere believing that he was capable of caring for the child.
Cross-examination of the parties do not enable me to form a view about whether the father abused the child in August 2022 or whether his parenting of the child was less than satisfactory. My assessment of the competing allegations was also compromised by the mother’s unexplained failure not to provide evidence in the form of an affidavit in the name of the maternal grandmother.
On balance, I am unable to make a finding that the father perpetrated family violence on the occasion alleged in August 2022.

Alleged sexual assault in April 2023

The mother says that during April 2023, and in circumstances where the parties’ daughter had been sleeping with the mother, the father “suddenly jumped on to my bed and climbed on top of me”. Thereafter, the mother says that:
He stripped me with force and I was fighting back, my body was bruises everywhere I was in so much pain. I begged him to please stop I would scream out loud so that the entire house will hear, and our baby will wake up and my mother will come into this room to help me and will report to the police. Only then he stopped and push me away.[64]
The mother also says that:
(a) Her “body was full of bruises due to [the father’s] attempted rape”.[65] I was not provided with any evidence that the mother’s body was “full of bruises” as so alleged. The mother did not report the alleged assault to police and for reasons not explained, the mother did not call the maternal grandmother who had been living with the parties at the time to give evidence; and

(b) On the same evening, she messaged the man who now describes himself as her partner to advise him what had occurred. He had been working in Country G at the time.[66]

The Court Child Expert recorded the following with respect to the mother’s allegation:
She was clear in her assertion that he forced her to have sex, although she demanded that he stop. [The mother] stated that this was the only time this occurred.[67]
Despite the gravity of the mother’s allegation, she did not mention it in the first affidavit, which she filed in relation to parenting proceedings on 15 September 2023 nor did the mother consider that the father represented a risk to the child. This is because, as discussed below, the mother as at September 2023 promoted unsupervised time between the child and the father.
The mother’s allegation was also brought into question as a consequence of cross-examination by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer. Particularly, counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer invited the mother to consider the content of her first affidavit filed on 15 September 2023 wherein the mother said as follows:
Overall the applicant was very controlling. He was not physically violent with me, however, at times he would become verbally aggressive, yelling at me for anything that was not done to his satisfaction.[68]
The concession made by the mother in September 2023 to the effect that the father was not violent with her, causes me to harbour doubt that the father was violent toward her in April 2023 as so alleged. Further, and when the mother attended on police in June 2023 to report the assault, her complaint recorded that the father performed oral sex.[69] There is no mention whatsoever of any penetration as now alleged.
The father denies the allegation of sexual assault in totality. In support of that denial, the father says that he was not aware of any bruises on the mother’s body nor did the mother report the alleged incident to New South Wales Police.[70] Similarly, and on cross-examination, the father:
(a) Denied that he pinned the mother down with force as alleged;

(b) Denied that he removed the mother’s clothing;

(c) Denied that the mother begged him to stop having sex with her; and

(d) Denied that he forced the mother to have sexual relations with him. In fact, the father denies having sex at all.

The father also advised the Court Child Expert that subsequent to the parties’ separation in March 2023 he did “not wish to have sex with [the mother] anymore”. He describes himself as being “upset and hurt” on learning that the mother met another man.[71]
In support of her contention, the mother produced text messages, which she exchanged with her partner. Those messages are undated but having initially been written in Country G language, were translated to English. The English version was marked as an exhibit.[72]
The text messages are expressed by the mother’s partner to be dated 20 March 2023. The mother says that the incident occurred in April 2023. I assume that the mother’s partner as made an error, but he was not cross-examined in relation to the same. In part, the messages read as follows:
Mother: He was trying to have sex with me.

I just escaped and ran into the toilet.

I’ve never been in so much pain.

Mother’s partner: Kick him in his face.

Did he do anything to you?

Mother: He tried to kiss me, but I pushed him away harder.

He pinned me down painfully.

I cried.

Does anyone understand me?

He asked me to hit him.

The harder I hit him.

The more I fought back, the more he tried to pin me down.

Mother’s partner: Kick him in his face.

Mother: My whole body hurts, and my private parts too.

How can I escape?

Mother’s partner: Bite him.
The content of the text messages between the mother and her partner on their face suggest that in April 2023 the mother was assaulted by the father. Indeed, the mother’s partner describes himself speaking to the mother by Facetime on the evening in question and describes the mother as being “very emotional and crying”.[73] I considered him to be an impressive witness. He gave his evidence in a straightforward and persuasive manner.
For the reasons discussed above, however, I am unable to find on the balance of probabilities that the father sexually assaulted the mother as so alleged. Equally, I am unable to find on the balance of probabilities that the father physically assaulted the mother in April 2023. The veracity of that statement is brought into question by the content of the mother’s affidavit filed in September 2023 (supra). The text messages referred to above, however, do support a conclusion that an act of family violence occurred on the evening in April 2023.

Surveillance of the mother

The mother says that in late March 2023, she located eight “hidden cameras” at the former matrimonial home. She says that cameras had been “implanted” in her bedroom, the bedroom of the maternal grandmother, the lounge room, the kitchen, the garage and the front of the house. She also says that a camera was installed in her motor vehicle.[74]
The mother says that about one week after her discovery, she confronted the husband. On the mother’s version of events, she tolerated the situation until mid to late June 2023, when tired of what she describes as the father’s “controlling behaviour” she asked the father to remove the cameras from the house and her motor vehicle. When the father declined to do so, the mother damaged them and was subsequently charged with causing property damage.[75] As discussed, the charges against the mother were withdrawn in March 2024.[76]
The father says as follows with respect to the installation of cameras by him:
There were cameras installed in visible places around the house, however there was 5 only. None of which were in the bedroom. I installed the cameras because I was concerned that [the mother] was implicit with illegal activities and drug dealing. She hung around people well known with marijuana plantation and distribution in [Suburb E].
The father on enquiry by the Court Child Expert gave a different explanation for the installation of the cameras. Particularly, the Court Child Expert records the following:
[The] father stated that he installed cameras in the home because he likes to see his daughter. When he was asked about privacy concerns, he asserted “it’s my house!”

...

He said that, after returning from [Country G], he replayed some of the videos, and heard and saw [the mother] being abusive to [the child]. [The father] said that [the mother] was yelling at [the child]. He said that [the mother] was also talking to her boyfriend.[77]
Then, and on cross-examination, the father said that he installed the cameras in March 2023 after there had been “robberies around the area” of the former matrimonial home and because there were steps inside the former matrimonial home, which may pose a risk to the child. He denied, however, that he installed the cameras in bedrooms. He also conceded that:
(a) He had sent video footage to a mutual friend of the parties from whom he sought advice about alleged abuse of the child by the mother;[78]

(b) Although the cameras were initially only set up to record outside the home, he altered the settings so that he could hear what was occurring inside and outside the home. The father says that he did so because he was concerned for the child’s safety;[79] and

(c) He installed the cameras for “many reasons” including to obtain information to verify his suspicion that the mother was having an affair.

Given the differing explanations given by the father with respect to his installation of surveillance equipment, and in circumstances where he conceded that the cameras were installed after he says that the mother had an affair and after the parties had separated under the same roof, I find that on balance, the father installed the cameras so as to monitor the mother’s movements and the mother’s conversations. For this reason, I also reject the father’s assertion that he was “generally unaware of the privacy issues that may ensue”.[80] When arriving at such a conclusion, I also bring to account the fact that:
(a) If the father was concerned that the stairs at the property presented a risk to the child, then it is surprising he did not install the cameras earlier. Indeed, by the time he says he installed the cameras, the child was two years of age;

(b) The father conceded by his own written material that he changed the settings of the cameras so that he could better monitor the mother’s treatment of the child; and

(c) The father’s application since the commencement of these proceedings has been that the child live with the mother and spend time with him.[81] This being so, it is difficult to understand what purpose the cameras could have served apart from monitoring the mother in circumstances where a few months after the installation of the cameras the father was by the terms of his application advising the Court that the mother was a parent who ought to retain primary care of the child.

In any event, Pickford & Pickford [2024] is now binding authority for the proposition that a perpetrator’s intention is not mandatory to proving family violence. As Justices Austin and Williams explained, family violence can occur where the perpetrator’s behaviour causes the victim to be “fearful” shifting the “attention to the subjective effect of the perpetrator’s behaviour upon the victim and away from the objective characterisation of the perpetrator’s conduct”.[82] In this case, the mother said that she felt that she was continually controlled so much so that she was aware that the father was following her “every move”. This being so, I accept that the father perpetrated family violence by taking steps to monitor the mother by electronic means. I also find that the father’s behaviour as measured by an objective standard necessarily constitutes family violence within the meaning of s 4AB of the Act.
Finally, counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer asked the father whether he had any regrets about installing the cameras particularly in circumstances where the child “would have been aware of the tension and anxiety that surveillance creates in a household” thereby making her “feel safe and insecure”.[83] In response, the father said he had no regrets. His answer causes me to hold a concern that the father has developed no insight whatsoever as to the consequences of his behaviour on his daughter and the relationship between himself and the mother.

Assault in September 2024

The mother says that in September 2024, she prepared the child for time with the father but says that the child “refused and cried a lot” and otherwise resisted transitioning into the care of the father.[84] On cross-examination, the father conceded that the child had presented in a similar manner for “two or three weeks in a row”.
The mother says that she made efforts to encourage the child, but when the child refused to do so, the father called her “a whore” and otherwise asserted that the mother did not know how to raise a child. She says that whilst arguing, the father “raised his hand up” and “held his first firm as if he was ready to hit me”.[85] She says that she immediately picked up the child “and moved away from [the father’s] striking zone”.[86] The mother then contacted her partner who took her to the K Police Station.[87] The father similarly attended.
The father denied on cross-examination by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer that he became angry. Indeed, the father said that he knew the parties “could not have an argument or fight or attack each other” because proceedings in this Court were on foot. I reject that evidence in circumstances where each party was so heightened by their exchange that each of them attended at a police station. Regrettably, the child was exposed to the interaction between the parties. On cross-examination, the father expressed regret about having attended the police station in September 2024 and having continued an argument at that location in the presence of the child.
The father denies that he was given a warning by any police officer at the K Police Station.[88] I accept the father’s assertion because it appears that unsatisfied by the actions of officers at the K Police Station, the mother attended the L Police Station. She says that she did so in circumstances where she “felt like I needed to do more to protect me and my daughter”.[89] Ultimately, and as a consequence of the mother’s efforts at the L Police Station, the father was charged with assault in October 2024.[90] On the same day, police issued an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order, which identified the father as a defendant.[91] The charges against the father were withdrawn in April 2025.[92]
The father by his written evidence denied the allegation in its totality. He said as follows:
It never happened when I come to pick up [the child], it is like every other Saturday. I never shout, I never raise my fists. I am quite [sic], I never like to upset my daughter, I always let her make her own decisions and respect her choice.[93]
The mother produced two photographs, which are endorsed as having been taken at 9.52am on [...] September 2024 being eight minutes prior to the commencement of time afforded to the father by the September 2023 orders.[94] The photographs depict the child in colourful clothing with pink hair ties, black leggings and black shoes. The mother also produced two photographs, which are endorsed as having been taken at 9.57pm in October 2024,[95] which as discussed above, represents the last occasion on which the father made effort to collect the child pursuant to the September 2023 orders. Those photographs identify the child wearing a pink skirt, pink leggings and black shoes. She was dressed in the same manner as depicted in video footage, which the father says he took on [...] September 2024.[96] That footage was only produced on the first or second day of trial. The implication of the mother’s evidence is that the father had as part of his defence of the criminal charges against him, produced video footage, which related to the events of October 2024 and not September 2024 thereby misleading the Court. I was not provided with any metadata, which may have enabled me to resolve the competing contentions of the parties.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer produced a record from New South Wales Police, which records the attendance of each party at the K Police Station at 10.30am on the day in question.[97] The record, which identifies the mother as the victim, reveals that each party made efforts to give his/her version of events to police. The relevant officer describes each party as acting in a “very immature” manner so much so that police were required to separate them. In part, the father’s narrative as identified by the record is as follows:
At 10am on [...] the [...] of September 2024, the [father] comes out of his vehicle and observes the [mother] bring [the child] out of the house and walking towards him. [The child] tells [the father] that she does not wish to go with him as she wants to stay with [the mother]. Due to this, the [father] got upset and felt as though that [sic] [the mother] has been brainwashing [the child] to make her hate the [father]. The [father] confronts the [mother] about this and tells her that she “feeds their child” stories and information to make her hate him. An argument ensues as a result of this. No threats, intimidations [sic] or assaults happened. The argument lasted 2 – 3 minutes before the [father] informed [the mother] that he will attend LOC to report a breach of the Family Law Court Order. The [mother] also tells [the father] that she will attend LOC to report him for starting an argument.
The record also identifies that police spoke to the mother who said as follows:
Police spoke with [the mother] first who provided the above version but stated the argument happened inside the house whilst she was sat down on the louneg [sic] and that she did not rehearse phrases with the child and she did not feed her information to say, she noted no threats or assaults, just an argument over a short period of time where the [father] labelled her a “prostitute”. Police noted the [mother] to be very argumentative and does not listen when spoken to by police.
On being told that an offence had not been committed, police record that the mother changed her version of events and said that the incident occurred outside the house. She said that she wished to obtain an apprehended domestic violence order, but police said that if she wished to do so she would need to be make efforts to obtain such an order independently.[98]
On cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer, the father said that whilst arguing outside the mother’s residence, the parties were standing about one metre apart in the presence of the child. He denied referring to the mother as a “prostitute” and denied making any other derogatory comments. I find those denials difficult to believe in circumstances where the father’s evidence was to the effect that the mother had abandoned him for a “party lifestyle”. It was also clear having observed the father across several days that he holds significant animus towards the mother on account of what he considered to be the mother’s infidelity. It was my observation that such animus has not dissipated with the effluxion of time. For these reasons, I am satisfied that the father at the very least denigrated the mother on [...] September 2024 in the presence of the child. Accordingly, and for the reasons described above, I am also satisfied that the child was exposed to family violence that day. The evidence presented, however, does not enable me to find that the father assaulted the mother as so alleged.
I am also satisfied that the child was exposed to the parties’ behaviour at the K Police Station given the father’s concession on cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer that the child was present. I consider that such a circumstance reflects poorly on each party particularly given that the mother could have readily left the child with her partner who as discussed drove the mother to the police station. I find that each party on this occasion failed to act in a manner consistent with the child’s best interests and/or shield the child from conflict between them.

Allegations of financial control

The mother says that in December 2018, she applied to work for a shop at Suburb E and was paid in cash. She says that the father asked her to give him the money and he would transfer monies back to her to pay for groceries. This mother says that this arrangement existed in circumstances where the father advised her that he “already declared tax for me”.[99]
Having made this allegation, the mother also says that:
(a) From 2017 to 2019, the parties “worked and saved as much money as possible with a plan to purchase” their own home. She says that by 2019, the parties were in a position to do so and had saved about ten percent of the loan amount;[100] and

(b) Although the parties obtained a loan from the Westpac Banking Corporation, the property was registered in the husband’s name.[101]

In response to the mother’s allegations, the father says that any money earned by the mother was deposited into an account in her name such that by 2019, the mother was able to purchase a luxury motor vehicle in her own name. He otherwise said that he did not know about any tax avoidance strategy implemented by the mother as so asserted.[102]
With respect to the purchase of the former matrimonial home, the father denied that the mother made any direct financial contribution and said that the mother’s name could not be on the title of the property because at the time its purchase, she was not a permanent resident.[103] He says that he financed the purchase of the property by using a company in his control as the guarantor for the loan.[104]
The mother’s allegations as to financial abuse are tempered by:
(a) The mother’s assertion through her counsel that during the relationship, the father paid her a “wage”;

(b) The mother’s evidence on cross-examination by the father’s counsel that subsequent to her emigration to Australia in 2017, she earned her own monies and each party had their own monies to contribute to holidays;

(c) The mother’s evidence that when she remained in Country G in early 2020 due to medical treatment and the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic, the father would send her monies so that she might meet her expenses of daily living;[105]

(d) The father’s evidence that he gave the mother about $300 per week for groceries.[106] By his oral evidence, the father says that on occasion he would give the mother more monies;

(e) The mother’s concession by her written evidence that during the period July 2021 to December 2022 the father was giving her about $700 per week “for my spending and sometimes food”.[107] The mother whilst acknowledging the receipt of these monies says that the father was only able to pay such a weekly sum due to benefits, which the father was receiving from the Commonwealth of Australia during the Covid-19 pandemic.[108] This might be so but nevertheless, the monies were forwarded to the mother by the father;

(f) The mother’s evidence that when the father left the mother and the child in Country G in January 2023, he again sent the mother monies despite the fact that the parties’ marriage had deteriorated as a consequence of the mother’s refusal to undergo in vitro fertilisation so that the parties might have a further child.[109] The father denies that the parties’ relationship deteriorated as a consequence of an argument about this topic. Instead, the father says that the relationship deteriorated because the mother was absent from the family socialising with the man who now describes himself as the mother’s partner;[110]

(g) The mother’s evidence that she was engaged in the father’s business doing “bookwork” and “ensuring that the books were balanced”.[111] There was some controversy during the mother’s cross-examination about whether the mother had an intimate understand of the father’s business or whether she relied on him for the accuracy of the information, which was being provided. Whatever the scenario, I find it difficult to understand how the mother would not by dint of the work being performed by her have at the very least a rudimentary understanding of the father’s business dealings and financial position. On its face, this contraindicates a finding that the mother was financially abused by the father;

(h) The father’s unchallenged evidence that during the parties’ relationship he regularly sent monies to the maternal grandparents and the maternal uncle who remained living in Country G as a gesture of goodwill;[112] and

(i) The father’s unchallenged evidence that he paid hospital fees for the maternal grandfather and subsequent funeral in early 2023.[113]

On balance, the evidence does not satisfy me that during the relationship, the father behaved in a manner, which constitutes financial abuse. Indeed, the facts summarised above cause me to form a contrary view. My opinion is fortified by the terms of a statutory declaration, which the father prepared in support of an application for a partner visa by the mother.[114] That document identifies that the mother assisted the father with his business and that the father paid her a salary. The matters referred to by the father in the Statutory Declaration are also consistent with the mother’s evidence (supra) that the father would meet the payment of groceries and other household expenses.
In the period subsequent to separation, however, the father accepted that he had not paid any monies to the mother by way of child support for the child. The father offered an excuse, namely, that he did not do so because the mother “did not ask for it”. This is despite the father’s concession on cross-examination that during the period 4 April 2022 to 15 June 2022, he paid to a credit card in his name a sum of $24,500.[115] Similarly, and during the period 1 July 2022 to 30 September 2022, the father conceded that he applied a sum of $17,500 to his credit card.[116] The father was not cross-examined with respect to the purpose of these transactions.
Several other aspects of the father’s behaviour in the period subsequent to separation cause me concern.
Firstly, the parties attended a mediation with respect to parenting issues on 20 September 2023. The mediation occurred in circumstances where:
(a) On 15 September 2023, the mother filed a Response to an Initiating Application at which time, the mother also made an application under Part VIII of the Act. On the same day, the mother filed a Financial Statement asserting that she held a 50 percent equitable interest in the former matrimonial home, which was held in the husband’s sole name;[117]

(b) Coincidingly, during September, the husband entered into a Contract for the Sale and Purchase of the former matrimonial home; and

(c) Settlement was due to occur in October 2023.[118]

On the sale of the property, the father dissipated the proceeds of sale in a manner not known to the mother.[119]
The Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) provides a definition of family violence at s 4AB. Sub-section (1) provides:
For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.
Sub-section (2) provides a non-exhaustive list of behaviours which may meet the definition.
In order for the father’s conduct as described above to meet the definition of family violence, it would need to satisfy the other aspects of s 4AB(1). That is it must be:
(a) Threatening in nature; or

(b) Behaviour which coerces or controls; or

(c) Behaviour which causes the mother to be fearful.

I am not satisfied that the father’s behaviour meets the definition of family violence under s4AB of the Act. However, the father’s behaviour is relevant to the orders to be made by me because it gives my insight as to the father’s ability to parent. Particularly, I consider that the father by combination of his actions as described above and his failure to pay child support to the mother demonstrated an unsatisfactory attitude towards parenting.

Conclusion as to risk presented by the father on account of family violence

I have concluded above that on balance, the father perpetrated family violence on the evening of [...] April 2023 and perpetrated family violence by installing CCTV cameras at the former matrimonial home. I have also found that each of the parties exposed the child to conflict between them in September 2024. This said, the allegations at their highest, individually or as a whole does not enable me to find that the level of the predicted risk to the child if she is to spend time with her father is unacceptable.
The mother’s allegations about the risks presented by the father are confusing. For example, the mother advised the Court Child Expert on 15 May 2024 that the child ought to “make the ultimate decision about spending overnight time with [the father]”.[120] It is difficult to understand why the mother would promote such an outcome if, in fact, she considered that the father presents a risk to the child.
Further, and when the mother filed a Response to Initiating Application on 15 September 2023 with the benefit of legal assistance, the mother promoted:
(a) An order to the effect that the parties have equal shared parental responsibility for the child;

(b) An order that until the age of four years, the child spend time with the father on each alternate Saturday and Sunday from 10.00am to 4.00pm; and

(c) An order that once the child attains the age of five years, the child spend time with the father on each alternate weekend from the conclusion of school on Friday (or 3.00pm if school is not in session) to 4.00pm on Sunday.

Counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer also cross-examined the mother about her answers as identified in a Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed on 15 September 2023. At Part C of that document, the mother denied that the child had been abused or at risk of abuse by the father. In response to a direct question, the mother confirmed that the content of the Notice was accurate.
Further, and as discussed above, the mother consented to an order on 29 September 2023 to the effect that commencing immediately, the children spend time with the father each Saturday from 10.00am to 4.30pm. Thereafter, and after a period of three months, the mother consented to an order that the child spend time with her father on each alternate weekend on both Saturday and Sunday from 10.00am to 4.30pm.
On cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer, the mother agreed that as at September 2023 she wanted her daughter “to have more time with the father, for them to have more contact”. She said that it “wasn’t until the changeover problems started” that she revised her position.
Similarly, I learned during the trial that:
(a) When the mother file an Amended Response to Initiating Application on 7 March 2024, she sought an order that the child spend time with her father each Saturday from 11.00am to 5.00pm;

(b) When the mother filed a document described as an Amended Response to Initiating Application on 17 April 2024, she again promoted orders for unsupervised time between the child and her father.

It necessarily follows that at all times between as September 2023 and April 2024, the mother considered that the father was a good enough parent to spend unsupervised time with the child. By that juncture, each of the acts of family violence alleged by the mother except the alleged assault of September 2024 had occurred. As discussed above, I am not persuaded that the father assaulted the mother on that occasion.
Save for the exposure of the child to conflict between the parties, the evidence falls short of establishing that the child is at any real risk of being subjected to family violence (either physically or psychologically) if she is to spend time with the father. Indeed, on cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer, the mother indicated that when the child attains the age of seven years, and provided she expresses a wish to do so, then “that would be ok”. It is difficult to reconcile that evidence with an allegation that the father presents a risk to the child on account of family violence.

Does the father present a risk of harm of to the child on the basis that he lacks parenting capacity?

The mother says that the father was disconnected from the daily responsibilities associated with caring for a newborn child. For instance, she says that the father “never changed a diaper for [the child], not even once”[121] and says that the father never attended to the child’s laundry.[122]
The mother’s allegation is contested by the father. Particularly, and whilst acknowledging that he worked between 6.00am and 4.00pm on five days each week, he says that he actively cared for the child on his return home until she was ready for bed at 9.00pm.[123] He also advised the Court Child Expert that he would spend time caring for the child on weekends.[124]
It is against this background that the mother says that she does not want the child spending overnight time with the father “because she has never spent time away from [the mother] overnight and would find this frightening”. The mother gave further detail explaining the child sleeps with her every night.[125]
As discussed, the mother’s allegations with respect to the father’s parenting capacity find form in the following specific allegations, namely, that:
(a) On 7 October 2023, and having spent the day with the father, she noticed a “long scratch” on the child’s buttocks. The child allegedly disclosed to her mother that she injured herself but that her father ignored the injury;[126]

(b) On [...] November 2023, and in circumstances where the child was ill, the mother asked the father whether he would forego his time with the child pursuant to the September 2023 orders (supra). The father would not agree, and the mother says that the father then ignored her various efforts across the course of the day to enquire as to the welfare of the child.[127] The mother says that on the evening of [...] November 2023 she took the child to a medical practitioner because she had pain in her abdomen. I discuss the mother’s criticisms of the father’s behaviour on this occasion in further detail below;

(c) On 16 March 2024, the child returned to her care with a bruise in the area of her genitalia. The child subsequently disclosed that she hurt herself on a slide whilst playing with the father but as discussed below, the mother was critical that the father did not bring the injury to her attention.[128]

Scratch on the child’s buttocks – 7 October 2023

On 7 October 2023, and having spent the day with the father, the mother says that she noticed a “long scratch” on the child’s buttocks when preparing the child for a shower. The child allegedly disclosed to her mother that she injured herself but that her father ignored the injury.[129]
On cross-examination, the father says that the child never complained of suffering any injury during her time with him.
The evidence as presented does not permit a finding that the father did anything on 7 October 2023, which is suggestive of a conclusion that he lacks parenting capacity or otherwise neglects the child. As I discuss below, children sustain injuries as an incidence of daily life.

Events of November 2023

As discussed above, the mother says that in November 2023 the child presented as tired and suffering from a “runny nose and sometimes [a] stomach ache”.[130] This being so, the mother asked the father whether he would forego his time with the child pursuant to the September 2023 orders (supra).
The father accepted on cross-examination that he did not speak to the mother during the hours of 10.00am and 4.30pm being the time afforded to him by the September 2023 orders despite the mother calling him “many times” to check on the child’s welfare.[131] The father gave contrary written evidence.[132]
In response to a question about whether he returned the child late that day in breach of the orders made in September 2023, the father was unable to recall and suggested that perhaps the child had been sleeping.
With respect to the child’s condition at the conclusion of time spending, the father says that the mother never expressed a concern about any medical condition suffered by the child at changeover instead saying that the mother only raised concern about the child suffering from a stomach ache about three hours later.[133] In a general sense, that evidence accords with the mother’s written evidence whereby she says that at about 8.00pm, the child began suffering from stomach pain and that this being so, she took the child to a doctor.[134]
The mother says that whilst attending on a medical practitioner one evening in November 2023, the father failed to respond to her enquiries about what the child had consumed that day.[135] The effect of the father’s evidence on cross-examination was that he had to attend to his work duties and this being so, I infer that perhaps the father was not as attentive to the mother’s enquiries as he ought to have been.
The child continued to suffer from stomach pain on the following morning and this being so, the mother took the child to the M Hospital where she was hospitalised for two nights and was found to have an intussusception of the bowel.[136] The mother makes complaint that having alerted the father to the child’s plight, he failed to make any enquiry as to the child’s welfare during her hospitalisation.[137]
Insofar as the mother says that the father failed to enquire as to the child’s welfare during her hospitalisation, the father says that the mother did not advise him that the child required medical attention until four days after the event.[138] Neither party produced any evidence of communications between them, which may have enabled me to determine the accuracy (or otherwise) of the competing contentions.
On balance, I consider the events of November 2023 to be unfortunate. It was not child focussed for the father to insist on spending time with the child in circumstances where the mother had raised the child’s illness with him. However, I accept that the father made such a decision in the context of limited time with the child pursuant to orders made in September 2023. The father did not by his affidavit respond to the mother’s allegation that he failed to respond to her queries about what the child had consumed during her time with him so that appropriate information might be conveyed to the medical practitioner treating the child.[139] Instead, and on cross-examination he said that he may have been distracted by his work duties. This being so and having regard to the precision of the mother’s evidence, I find that the father acted in a less than satisfactory manner in November 2023. On balance, I do not consider that he responded to the mother’s enquiries of the child’s welfare in a timely manner or in a manner focused on the best interests of the child. As I will discuss, however, I suspect that the events of November 2023 were exacerbated by the inability of the parties to support each other’s parenting of the child. I find that the father’s behaviour on that occasion (and the mother’s response to such behaviour) was motivated by the animus, which each parent holds in relation to the other of them.

Bruising sustained 16 March 2024

On 16 March 2024, the mother says that the child returned to her care with a bruise in the area of her genitalia. The mother says that she identified the bruise when getting the child ready for a shower. The child subsequently disclosed that she hurt herself on a slide whilst playing with the father and that having done so, the father did not provide her with appropriate attention.[140] The mother also makes complaint that the father did not bring the injury to her attention.[141]
On cross-examination, the father accepted that the mother sent him a photograph of the injury by text message but said that he did not know that the child suffered any injury. Particularly, the father said that the child did not express that she was suffering from any pain nor did she point at the area during her time with him.
On enquiry by the Court Child Expert, the father said as follows:
[The father] said that [the mother] sends a lot of texts, and that she alleged that [the child] had bruises on her private parts. He said that he does not undo [the child’s] pants to check such things, and that he is sure that [the child] would tell him if she were hurt.[142]
For her part, the mother advised the Court Child Expert that she did not consider that the father “somehow contributed to the bruise” instead complaining that she was “left unaware about what had happened”.[143]
On balance, I decline having regard to the parties’ written evidence or evidence elicited on cross-examination to find that the father did anything inappropriate on 16 March 2024 or that bruising of the child must necessarily mean that the father lacks parenting capacity or neglects the child. The mother herself concedes that the father did not “contribute” to the bruise. Further, young and active children sustain injuries as an incidence of daily life. It may be that the child did advise her father that she had suffered an injury. Even so, the injury appears to have been minor in nature and the only criticism, which might be made of the father is that he did not advise the mother of the same.

Conclusion as to the risk presented by the father to the child on account of his parenting capacity?

The events referred to above occurred on 7 October 2023, November 2023 and 16 March 2024.
As discussed above, I learned during the trial that:
(a) When the mother file an Amended Response to Initiating Application on 7 March 2024, she sought an order that the child spend time with her father each Saturday from 11.00am to 5.00pm; and

(b) When the mother filed a document described as an Amended Response to Initiating Application on 17 April 2024, she again promoted orders for unsupervised time between the child and her father.

It necessarily follows that despite her expressed concerns, the mother’s view must have been that the father was a good enough parent to spend time with the child on an unsupervised basis.
Despite being asked by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer and by me as to the reason why she amended her application on 28 May 2025 to seek an order that the child spend no time whatsoever or otherwise with the father, the mother was unable to identify a rational basis for the significant change. The only event identified by the mother, which could have caused such a change was the alleged assault of the mother by the father in September 2024. As discussed above, and although each party behaved poorly on that occasion, I have been unable to find that the father assaulted the mother as so alleged.
Again, and save for the exposure of the child to conflict between the parties, the evidence falls short of establishing that the child is at any real risk on account of the father’s parenting capacity.

Will the mother’s parenting capacity be adversely impacted if the Court makes an order for time spending between the child and her father?

The mother advised the Court Child Expert that she is “fearful” of the father.[144]
Similarly, and by her counsel’s Outline of Case Document, the mother says as follows:
(a) Contact with the father causes her anxiety and distress;[145] and

(b) If she is required to facilitate contact between the child and the father “it will cause her distress and anxiety and trauma impacting on her ability to care for the child”.[146]

In relation to whether or not the mother’s fear of the father might impact on the mother’s parenting capacity, the Court Child Expert said as follows:
