It is possible that Ms [Zain’s] parenting ability may be compromised if she is obliged to provide [X] to Mr [Sanket] for extended periods, and that this could result in Ms [Zain] feeling significantly anxious, something which may impact on [X]. It is therefore a matter of weighing the benefit to [X] of a relationship with her father and the risks which may be inherent in her having this relationship.[147]
During her oral evidence, the Court Child Expert also said:
If [the mother] is anxious about [the child] spending time with [the father], this will inevitably be transmitted to [the child] and could impair [the mother’s parenting capacity]. We don’t parent as well when we are anxious.
In Keane & Keane [2021] FamCAFC 1; (2021) 62 Fam LR 190 (“Keane”) the Full Court confirmed that the task is:
...to analyse carefully the evidence led on behalf of the mother in relation to the impact that allowing the child supervised time with the father would have on her caregiving capacity. For present purposes however it may be accepted that there would need to be very cogent evidence that, to use the language of the Full Court in Marra & Marra, the mother's caregiving capacity would be discernibly impaired by any order that the child have time with the father (see Re Andrew, at 83,201) for such an order not to be made. Ultimately, as the Full Court has consistently observed, the lodestar is the welfare and best interests of the child, which principle now finds statutory expression in s 60CA of the Act: Re Andrew, 83,199.
The Full Court in Keane at [75] summarised that the authorities relating to the “Re Andrews principle”. The impact of a genuinely held belief on the primary caregiver's ability to parent has been expressed in a variety of ways including:
“may so impinge upon her capacity as the primary caregiver of the children” [A & A [1998] FamCA 25; (1998) FLC 92-800];
“her co-parenting capacity would deteriorate and impinge the children’s best interests” [Dunst & Dunst [2016] FamCAFC 15 at 81];
“as her likely being unable to continue to function effectively so as to adequately care for the children” [Hollister & Gosselin [2016] FamCA 759 at 193];
“genuine fears of the residential parent about such a risk may so impinge upon the parent’s capacity” [Bayer & Imhoff [2010] FamCA 532 at 177];
“where such anxiety is likely to impact adversely on that parents caregiving ability...” [Russell & Russell v Close SA 45 of 1992 an unreported decision of the Full Court];
“have an effect on the custodial parent which will impair to a significant degree the emotional support and for that matter physical support which the custodial parent can render the child” [Grant & Grant (1994) FLC 92-506 at 81-259];
It was the oral evidence of the Court Child Expert that an order causing changeover to occur at a neutral venue would assist the parties to avoid conflict at handovers. The father on cross-examination agreed. Further, and notwithstanding her concerns, it remained the evidence of the Court Child Expert that subject to findings as to risk and exposure to parental conflict, it would be in the child’s best interests to recommence spending time with her father.[148] For the reasons identified above, I do not consider that the father represents a risk of harm to the child. Further, I accept the Court Child Expert’s evidence that conflict between the parties can be reduced by:
(a) An order providing the mother with sole decision-making responsibility for major decisions concerning the child;

(b) An order requiring the parties to perform changeovers at a neutral location; and

(c) At least initially, an order for daytime contact only.

CONCLUSION AS TO TIME SPENDING ARRANGEMENTS

The father agreed on cross-examination that the parties do not have an ability to communicate on any level conceding “between us we cannot talk”. Indeed, he said that at the time of trial, he had not communicated with the mother at for a period of about eleven months. As discussed above, the father when giving evidence presented as a person preoccupied with the mother’s alleged infidelity. He was unable to speak in a positive manner about the mother’s parenting capacity even when pressed by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
Whilst preoccupied by the mother’s conduct, it became clear to me that the father has limited insight in relation to the effect of his behaviour on the parenting dynamic between the parties and accordingly, his relationship with his daughter. As an example, the father gave evidence that he had not completed a parenting after separation course or the “Tuning into Kids and/or Circle of Security” programs as was so ordered by a Senior Judicial Registrar on 29 September 2023.[149] I consider the inability of the parties to communicate on any level whatsoever as well as the father’s inability to consider the effect of his behaviour on his relationship with his daughter to be a factor, which I need to bring to account when considering parenting arrangements, which are in the child’s best interests. Indeed, the Court Child Expert identified the animosity between the parties and the lack of functional communication between them as being “an impediment to a good and free relationship” between the child and each of her parents.
With respect to each party’s insight (or lack thereof), the Family Report Writer said as follows:
Neither parent appeared to value the relationship [the child] may have with the other. [The father] appeared somewhat preoccupied with [the mother’s] alleged infidelities, and was unable to speak positively about [the mother’s] parenting. [The mother] was similarly dismissive of [the father’s] parenting capacity, and seemed to wish to limit his time with [the child]. This may represent and inability to appreciate [the child’s] need to have two parents who are able to support each other in parenting her and to avoid exposing her to further conflict.[150]
I accept the Court Child Expert’s opinion that in probability, the child has discerned the animosity in her parents’ relationship, and such animosity and conflict has created in the child a loyalty conflict, which has made her feel as if she needs to choose a side.[151] There is a basis for such a finding. For example:
(a) The mother says that after the child spent time with the father on 16 March 2024, the child said “father told me that mommy is not a good person, and that everyone around mommy are bad [and] that I should hate them all”;[152]

(b) The father reported that the mother refused to refer to him as “daddy” subsequent to separation instead referring to him by his Christian name.[153] I conclude that if this occurred, it would indeed have caused the child confusion and anxiety;

(c) The mother advised the Court Child Expert that father had informed the child that her mother does not love her;[154]

(d) The mother’s partner advised the Court Child Expert that following time spending with the father, the child behaved “in an angry and challenging way”;[155] and

(e) The father advised the Court Child Expert that the child had “told him that he is ‘no good because he took the mother to jail”.[156]

It is against this background, that I find that a significant risk faced by the child is the conflict between her parents. Indeed, the Court Child Expert said as follows:
[The child] may find herself caught in a trap where loyalty to one parent implies denigration of the other. In this instance, [the child] may ultimately opt to remain loyal to just one parent as a means of avoiding the difficulties associated with maintaining a relationship with each. Or she may seek to manage the incongruity of having parents who love her but dislike each other by maintaining an attitude when she is with one parent which accords more nearly with that parent’s feelings and opinions. Neither is a healthy stance for [the child]. In the former instance, she risks losing her relationship with one of her parents, in the latter, she is required to maintain an inauthenticity, ‘to live a lie,’ which is ultimately damaging to her sense of self and the world.[157]
Having said this, and when giving her oral evidence on 28 November 2025, the Court Child Expert also advised me that I ought to be aware of the risks, which may ensure if the child’s relationship with her father is severed. As described by the Court Child Expert in her November 2025 report, the advantages of the child spending time with her father “would be an affirmation of her importance to her father and the reassurance of him having a presence in her life”.[158] Such an outcome would also avoid the disadvantages associated with a severance of the child’s relationship with her father. As described by the Court Child Expert, these disadvantages include a “possible over-idealisation” of her father as she ages, a sense of blame of herself and the formation of a view that she is “somehow lacking or unworthy”.[159]
On 15 May 2024, the Court Child Expert undertook an observation of interaction between the child and the father. Regrettably, an interpreter was not available on the day of the observation[160] and this being so, the observation occurred in circumstances where the Court Child Expert could not understand the verbal interactions between the child and her father. Nevertheless, the Court Child Expert recorded the following with respect to her observations:
[The child] was brought into the observation room by the Court Child Expert who had explained that she would be seeing ‘Daddy’. [The child] then looked surprised when she saw [the father], and immediately said “I want Mummy”. There was a short interval of apparent reluctance to engage with [the father], but [the child] quickly started talking with him and both commenced playing with the cars and garage in the room. [The child] spoke volubly in [Country G language], and she appeared confident and comfortable with [the father]. They both laughed and showed shared enjoyment over a game of quoits. [The child] laughed delightedly at something [the father] said and she exhibited good concentration on a task. Her language in [Country G language] appeared fluent. When it came time for [the father] to leave, [the child] did not want to say goodbye, and was only able to be returned to the childcare room after she was reassured that she would see [the father] at the weekend.[161] (emphasis added)
Having undertaken observations of interaction between the child and each of her parents, the Court Child Expert concluded that the child had “a warm and comfortable relationship with each of her parents”.[162] The mother on cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer agreed with that assessment.
On 7 October 2025, and at my request, the Court Child Expert undertook further observations of interaction between the child and each of her parents in circumstances where by that juncture, the child had not seen her father for about twelve months. With respect to that interaction, the Court Child Expert recorded the following:
[The child] was initially reluctant to enter the room where [the father] was sitting, and she was not responsive to [the father’s] questions. He asked her if she was going to school on that day and if she felt shy. [The child] shook her head before showing [the father] her watch, saying “I can ring Mum”. [The father] said, “maybe don’t ring now”, and he asked her if she wanted something to eat. [The child] shook her head and proceeded to drink water out of her bottle. She did not appear scared, however, did not respond to [the father’s] overtures. He asked her about the times she is taken to school and the time she arrives home, and he tried to engage with her. [The child] started talking a little more and there were some exchanges about the colours in a painting on the wall. [The father] then asked [the child] if she would like to do a drawing, and both sat at the table. [The child] became much more animated and opened up a good deal more. She told [the father] about the colours she has at home, and both chatted about her drawing. The atmosphere became more comfortable, and [the child] was laughing and talking to [the father] who responded appropriately. She was seen to be quite assertive when [the father] misidentified a character in her drawing, and immediately said “noooo’. Both continued in an amiable and relaxed manner. [The child] said “goodbye” when [the father] left and did not appear to be unduly disturbed by her encounter with him.[163] (emphasis added)
In my opinion, it is remarkable that the Court Child Expert continued to describe the relationship between the child and her father as “warm”[164] given the time, which had passed since the child last saw him. This being so, and in circumstances where save for parental conflict I have found that the father does not present a risk of harm to the child, I have concluded that I ought to make orders, which will preserve a relationship between the child and her father.
When doing so, I cannot ignore the events of September 2024 and the possible effects, which the conflict between the parties that day had on the child’s subsequent preparedness to spend time with the father. I accept that the exchange between the parties in September 2024 and their subsequent attendance at a police station together with the child may have undermined the child’s confidence in her father to act as a protective caregiver.[165] It is for this reason that I propose to proceed in the cautious manner described below.
With respect to the structure of time spending between the child and her father:
(a) The father conceded that it would not matter to him if he had to commence spending time with the child in a supervised environment. It was put to the father by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer that such an arrangement might be appropriate at least initially in circumstances where by the time the trial concluded, the child had not spent any time with the father for about fourteen months. Such an arrangement was supported by the Court Child Expert;[166]

(b) The father gave no evidence whatsoever about where the child might sleep or the facilities, which might be afford to her in circumstances where at the current juncture, the father is living with friends; and

(c) The Court Child Expert expressed a view that it would be in the child’s interests to spend daytime periods her father. She expressed a view that a progression to overnight time would need to be considered once the success of daytime periods was assessed.[167]

I am obligated to finalise the litigation on the available evidence. Building some form of self-executing mechanism into the final orders to commence overnight time at some indeterminate point in the future is arbitrary and may lead me falling into error by trying to conjure orders which do not match the evidence.[168] I would simply be speculating as to the success or otherwise of supervised time and daytime visits.
On balance, and for the reasons discussed above, I consider that it is in the child’s best interests to make orders in the following terms:
(a) For a period of six months, and in any event on no less than twelve occasions, the child spend supervised time with the father in each alternate week for no less than two hours on each occasion with such time to be supervised by the G Contact Centre at Suburb D in the State of New South Wales or such other contact service as may be agreed between the parties;

(b) Thereafter, and for a period of three months, on each Saturday from 10.00am to 1.00pm; and

(c) Thereafter on each Saturday from 10.00am to 4.00pm.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer made a submission to the effect that the time referred to at sub-paragraphs (a) and (b) above ought to be suspended on the first two Saturdays of the Term One, Term Two and Term Three school holidays so that the mother might be afforded an opportunity to travel with the child.
It is, of course, in the child’s interests to travel with her mother and/or to spend time with her mother in a relaxed manner during school holiday periods. Accordingly, I will make orders in the terms promoted by the Independent Children’s Lawyer.

SPECIFIC ISSUE ORDERS

Requirement to consult the father in relation to major long-term decisions

The father promoted an order to the effect that the mother be obliged to consult with the father prior to making any major long-term decisions in respect of any medical treatment or the child’s education.
In circumstances where I have concluded that the parties have no capacity whatsoever to communicate and in circumstances where each of the child’s parents holds significant animus towards the other, I do not consider the order promoted by the father to be appropriate. The making of such an order will only serve to cause conflict between the parties.
I will, however, make an order that the mother notify the father of any major long-term decision affecting the child within seven (7) days of making such a decision. I consider that such an order is in the child’s best interests given that by operation of my orders, the child will be spending time with the father.

Cost of supervised visits

The Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted an order that the father meet the payment of all costs associated with supervised time including the fees associated with any intake procedure and child orientation.
I was not addressed about the proposal of the Independent Children’s Lawyer by the father’s counsel. However, and in circumstances where the mother will by operation of my orders be charged with the primary care of the child, I consider it is appropriate to make an order that the father meet the costs associated with the child’s attendance at a contact service.

Time between the children and their father on special occasions

The Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted time between the child and her father on the occasion of the child’s birthday, Chinese New Year’s Eve and Father’s Day.
By dint of her application, the mother did not support the making of any order for time between the child and her father on special occasions. The father was otherwise silent as to such time.[169]
I am reluctant to make any order between the child and her father on the occasion of Chinese New Year’s Eve as I was not addressed as to the usual practice associated with such an occasion nor do I have any information about the mother’s usual practice.
I also decline to make an order for time between the child and her father on the occasion of the child’s birthday. I have arrived at this decision in circumstances where the relationship between the parties is poor. I do not consider it in the child’s interests to be subjected to a handover between her parents on a day, which ought to be enjoyable for her. The father will no doubt celebrate the child’s birthday within the limits of the time, which I have afforded him.
I will, however, make orders for time between the child and her father on the occasion of Father’s Day.

Changeovers

The father agreed on cross-examination that it is in the child’s best interests that the parties not come into contact with each other at changeover. Counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted an order that if possible, changeovers occur at the H Contact Centre at Suburb D or such other contact service as may be agreed between the parties. If a contact service is not available, then the Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted an outcome that changeovers occur in the carpark of a McDonald’s Restaurant.
For her part, the mother wished for handovers to occur at the residence of the man who by his affidavit, identified himself as the mother’s partner.[170] Given the animus between the parties and the father’s resentment about the fact that the mother established a new relationship, I do not consider that it is in the child’s best interests for changeovers to occur in the manner proposed by the mother.
For her part, the Court Child Expert expressed a view that the child may be protected from hostility and acrimony between her parents if changeover occurs at a contact service. I agree that such an arrangement is appropriate. I will also make orders to cater for the possibility that the contact service is closed on a day on which the child is to spend time with the father.

Communication between the child and her parents

The Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted an order that the child have telephone communication with the parent with whom she is not otherwise spending time each Tuesday and Thursday.[171] As a consequence of the orders to be made by me, it would necessarily follow that in reality, it would be the father making efforts to communicate with the child whilst the child is in the mother’s household.
I was not provided with any submission as to the appropriateness of such an order.
Given the animus between the parties, I consider that an order in the terms promoted by the Independent Children’s Lawyer has potential to create conflict between the parties. I will, however, make an order that the mother do all things to facilitate a telephone call between the child and her father in the event that the child requests such an opportunity at any reasonable time.

Communication between the parties

The Court Child Expert expressed concern that a lack of communication between the parties “will hamper consistency in the management of [the child’s] health, education and other activities”.[172]
In order to improve communication between the parties, the Court Child Expert promoted the use of a “parenting app”[173] and the Independent Children’s Lawyer adopted that proposal.
On cross-examination, the father said that he did not know how to use a “parenting app” but I find it difficult to understand how the father might be prejudiced in the long-term. That is, I expect he will be able to develop the relevant skills quite quickly.
For these reasons, I will adopt the proposal of the Independent Children’s Lawyer with respect to the use of a “parenting app”. I will not, however, require the parties to use any particular “app” as the cost and benefits of different “apps” was not the subject of evidence or submission.

Provision of medical information to each parent

The Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted an order that each parent keep the other advised of the identity of any medical practitioner and/or allied health professional attended on by the child and that otherwise, each party be at liberty to consult any medical practitioner and/or allied health professional attended on by the child. I consider it is important that each parent have this ability and accordingly, I will make orders in the terms promoted by the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
For the same reasons, I will also make an order requiring each parent to notify the other in the event that the child suffers from a significant injury or illness whilst in his/his care.

School events, school information and extracurricular activities

The Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted an order that upon the commencement of unsupervised time, the father be at liberty to attend at all school and extracurricular activities to which parents are ordinarily invited to attend. Regrettably, and given the animus between the parties, I do not consider that such an order is in the child’s best interests. Simply, the parties do not have the skills or maturity necessary to navigate complexities, which may arise if I made such an order. However, I will make an order, which permits the father to attend only at those school events and extracurricular activities, which fall during the child’s time with him.
I do consider it is important, however, that each parent be at liberty to obtain school reports and other information relevant to the child’s welfare, development and academic progress. This being so, I will make an order that each parent be at liberty to obtain copies of the child’s school reports, school notices and school photographs at each parent’s sole expense in all things.

Restraints

An injunction is not, by definition, a “parenting order”.[174] The Act provides separate powers for making injunctions, parentings orders, or other orders in relation to the welfare of a child.[175] Whilst counsel did not address me on the issue, the only power afforded to me to make the injunctive orders sought by the parties is pursuant to s 68B of the Act. Section 68B is not subject to the paramountcy principle — the words of the section lack any express reference thereto. This issue has been addressed in many authorities and, as summarised in Attwood v Attwood [2022] FedCFamC1F 6, has to some extent been reconciled:
... Section 68B(1) of the Act states that the Court may make such order or grant such injunction as it considers appropriate for the welfare of the child. Section 68B(2) also states that the making of the order may occur in any case in which it appears to the Court to be just or convenient to do so.
The Full Court in Bennett v Bennett (2001) FLC 93–088 determined that the power in s 68B was not subject to the express legislative requirement that the Court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration; and even if s 68B were subject to the “best interests” principle, it is doubtful that this would displace the established common law principles contained in the authorities such as In re Boaler [1915] 1 KB 21; Commonwealth Trading Bank v Inglis [1974] HCA 17; (1974) 131 CLR 311; Re Attorney-General (Cth); Ex parte Skyring [1996] HCA 4; (1996) 135 ALR 29 and Coco v R [1994] HCA 15; (1994) 179 CLR 427. However, the Full Court in Flanagan and Handcock (2001) FLC 93–074 considered this and referred to CDJ v VAJ (No 1) (1998) 197 CLR 172 which decided that orders that are not a parenting order do not directly invoke the application of the paramountcy principle. Nevertheless the Court agreed with the Full Court that the consideration of what is in the best interests of the child are “powerful matters to be weighed up against a competing principle such as finality”. The Full Court therefore held that in respect of the issue of an injunction under s 68B, it is incorrect to state that the “paramountcy principle” applies. However, the best interests principle needs to be given careful consideration, especially where the orders sought to be made intimately concern the welfare of the children.
(Emphasis added)
Thus, whilst the best interests of the child is not stated explicitly as the governing principle when making an injunction under s 68B, for all practical purposes the concept provides a useful framework within which to explore whether ... the injunction is appropriate for the welfare of the child, and is otherwise just or convenient[176].
Having regard to Attwood and Bennett, I will consider the injunctive relief sought by giving careful consideration to the best interests of the children.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer sought orders that the parties be restrained from:
(a) Speaking of or permitting any other person to speak to or about the other parent or his/her family in a critical or derogatory manner in the presence or hearing or the child or allowing any other person to do so;

(b) Physically disciplining the child or allowing any other person to do so;

(c) Consuming alcohol to excess of the legal limit for driving in the presence of the child.

The injunctive relief sought restraining each parent from denigrating the other parent to or in the presence of the child is, of course, in the best interests of the child. I will make orders in those terms. I also agree it is in the child’s best interests to make orders with respect to the use of physical discipline. There is, however, no evidentiary basis to make the order with respect to the consumption of alcohol.
By her Third Amended Response to Initiating Application filed on 28 May 2025, the mother seeks orders pursuant to s 68B of the Act in the following terms, namely, that the father be restrained from:
(a) Approaching within 100 metres of the mother;

(b) Making or attempting to make contact with the child including but not limited to the methods of post, telephone calls, video calls, emails, social media or electronic messaging service; and

(c) Attending at the child’s school or the mother’s residence.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer promoted orders in similar terms albeit the orders promoted by her were subject to the operation of the other parenting orders made by me.
Given the animus between the parties, I consider that it is in the best interests of the child to make orders in the terms referred to at sub-paragraphs (a) and (c) above subject however to the parenting orders to be made by me.
I decline to grant the injunctive relief referred to at sub-paragraph (b) above in circumstances where by dint of my orders, the child will maintain a relationship with her father.

Travel outside of the Commonwealth of Australia

The mother seeks an order that:
(a) She be permitted to apply for the issue of an Australian Passport in the name of the child absent the consent of the mother. The father consents to an order that the mother be permitted to do so and that the mother otherwise retain the passport in her possession;[177]

(b) She be permitted to travel outside of the Commonwealth of Australia with the child.

On cross-examination by the Independent Children’s Lawyer the father conceded that he was comfortable with the prospect of the mother removing the child from the Commonwealth of Australia for the purposes of travel to a country, which is a signatory to the Convention of the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (“the Hague Convention”). By his Case Outline Document, the father also indicated that he consents to the removal of the child’s name from the Family Law Watch List in place at all ports of arrival and departure in Australia.
The question, which then arises is whether the mother be permitted to travel with the child to Country G, which is not a signatory to the Hague Convention.
I refer to the well-known decision of Line & Line [1996] FamCA 145; (1996) 21 Fam LR 259, which has been followed and applied on many occasions. In that decision, the Full Court set out the type of considerations to be considered when such a parenting order for overseas travel is made. Having regard to Line, I note the following:
(c) The child is an Australian Citizen;

(d) I was advised during Closing Submissions that the mother recently became an Australian Citizen;

(e) The mother is unemployed and in receipt of Commonwealth benefits;[178]

(f) The mother has no assets in Australia whatsoever[179] save that by orders made with the consent of the parties on 26 November 2025, the mother was to receive a sum of about $100,000.

(g) The man who describes himself in his affidavit as the mother’s partner, has a number of business interests in Country G;[180] and

(h) Members of the maternal family reside in Country G.

The mother expressed a wish to be able to travel to Country G with the child, so that she is able to see her relatives and be exposed to her culture.[181] For her part, the Court Child Expert expressed a view that it would be important for the child to “enjoy the culture of her family’s origin...and if a means can be found to do this safely, then it would be recommended that this occur”.[182]
The Independent Children’s Lawyer did not promote an order in the terms sought by the mother, nor did he promote any order for the mother to travel with the child outside of Australia.
I agree with the opinion expressed by the Court Child Expert to the effect that it would be in the child’s interests to see her relatives and be exposed to her culture. I am, however, concerned that the relationship between the parties is such that the mother has a motive not to return to Australia with the child. This being so, and in order to ameliorate the risk of the child not returning to Australia, I will require the mother to pay to her solicitor’s trust account a bond of $7,500 not less than seven days prior to travelling with such sum to be refunded to the mother within seven days of her return to Australia. In circumstances where the mother has nominal assets, a loss of $7,500 will be of significance to the mother and in my opinion, is an amount sufficient to incentivise compliance with the orders.
Additionally, and as sought by the father, I will make orders for the mother to provide the father with written notice of her intention to travel with the child outside of the Commonwealth of Australia, providing the following details:
(a) Departure and return dates;

(b) Mode of travel including airline tickets or cruise tickets;

(c) Details in respect of the places of accommodation including addresses and telephone numbers; and

(d) In the event that the mother is not staying in commercial accommodation, she is to provide the contact details for the friend or relative with whom she is staying.

I decline to make orders providing the father with make up time in the event that the period of travel overlaps with time, which the child is to be spending with the father pursuant to these Orders. As discussed in this Judgment, the parties’ ability to communicate is poor and I do not consider that the parties have the ability to negotiate make up time. However, I propose to limit the period of any travel overseas to a period of four weeks in each calendar year so that the child’s time with the father is not interrupted to any significant degree.

Application by the Independent Children’s Lawyer for costs

The Independent Children’s Lawyer made an application for the father to pay her costs fixed in a sum of $13,211.98.[183]
Section 114UB(1) of the Act abolishes for the purposes of family law proceedings, the general rule that, in civil proceedings, costs follow the event. It provides that each party should bear his or her own costs in such proceedings.
However, s 114UC(1) of the Act authorises the Court to make orders for the costs of an Independent Children’s Lawyer:
(1) In proceedings in which an independent children’s lawyer for a child has been appointed, the court may make a costs order, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise, to the effect that each party to the proceedings bears, in such proportion as the court considers just, the costs of the independent children’s lawyer in relation to the proceedings.
The power to make an order for costs in favour of an Independent Children’s is, however, subject to s 114UC(2) of the Act, which provides:
(2) However, if:

(a) a party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings; or

(b) the court considers that party to the proceedings would suffer financial hardship if the party had to bear a proportion of the costs of the independent children’s lawyer;

the court must not make an order against that party in relation to the costs of the independent children’s lawyer.
Accordingly, and with respect to the application made by the separate representative, it is only a question of discretion and whether that discretion should be exercised. Section 114UB(2) of the Act is clear[184] in that:
If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsection (3) of this section, sections 114UC and 114UD and the applicable rules of Court, make such order as to costs and security for costs, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise.
When considering what order (if any) is just, the legislation mandates that the court have regard to those matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act. The legislation does not identify that any of those seven matters is determinative. However, the Court must consider each matter in the seven alphabetical subsections.[185] There is nothing to prevent any factor being the sole foundation for any order for costs being made.
In the matter of Nardini & Legal Aid NSW [2019] FamCA 340, the Deputy Chief Justice considered issues relevant to the payment of costs of the ICL. Particularly, his Honour said as follows:
In terms of s 117(2A)(g), it is relevant that the role played by the ICL in parenting proceedings, including in this matter, is invaluable. The role of the ICL was summarised in the context of an application for costs in proceedings before the High Court of Australia in CDJ v VAJ (No 2) (1998) 197 CLR 172. Specifically, at [11], Kirby J said:
The children's representative has a duty to “act in an independent and unfettered way in the best interests of the child”. This duty carries over to an appeal. The interests of the children and their welfare is of concern to the public. Those interests extend beyond, and are separate from, the interests of the parents. The children are the children of both parties. They should share equally the costs of their children being separately represented in this court. [References omitted].
I accept that it is in the public interest for the best interests of children to be represented in proceedings before this Court and that the Court invariably receives substantial assistance, in that regard, from ICLs appointed in parenting proceedings. Such assistance was undoubtedly provided by the ICL in this case.
Also relevant to these proceedings is s 117(5) of the Act, which provides:

(5) In considering what order (if any) should be made under subsection (2) in proceedings in which an independent children's lawyer has been appointed, the court must disregard the fact that the independent children's lawyer is funded under a legal aid scheme or service established under a Commonwealth, State or Territory law or approved by the Attorney-General.
Accordingly, it is my view that the ICL should be presumed to be unfunded and, having regard to authority, in those circumstances, the Court is generally inclined to order litigants to contribute to the ICL’s costs: Gahen & Gahen (No 2) [2013] FamCA 936 and De Roma & De Roma [2013] FamCA 566.
Further, I note that the ICL has sought an order for costs to be paid by the mother in a lump sum amount. Those costs have been itemised in a schedule provided by the ICL to the Court and the mother at the hearing on 20 May 2019.
Pursuant to rule 19.18(1)(a) of the Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth), the Court may make an order for costs of a specific amount. Having regard to the principles adumbrated by Kent J in Stoian & Flemming (Costs) [2014] FamCA 944 at [91], I am satisfied that the costs figure sought by the ICL is logical, fair and reasonable. I will, therefore, make an order for costs in favour of the ICL in the sum of $14,903.70, being 50 per cent of the total costs incurred by the ICL in this matter.
At paragraph 22 of his decision, the Deputy Chief Justice referred to the decision of Kirby J in the matter of CDJ v VAJ (No 2) (1998) 197 CLR 172 (“CDJ v VAJ”), wherein his Honour said at [11]:
The children’s representative has a duty to “act in an independent and unfettered way in the best interests of the child”. This duty carries over to an appeal. The interests of the children and their welfare is of concern to the public. Those interests extend beyond, and are separate from, the interests of the parents. The children are the children of both parties. They should share equally the costs of their children being separately represented in this court.

(References omitted)
The father was in receipt of funding pursuant to the Family Violence and Cross-Examination of Parties Scheme. Prior to that time, the father’s Cost Notices indicate that the amount expended by him on legal fees was nominal. However, and as I discuss below, the father’s solicitor failed on two occasions to file Costs’ Notices as required by Rule 12.06 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021.
At the time of trial, the father was employed as a driver, but his income was modest. Further, the father’s assets were modest, and he described himself as being indebted to various third parties.
Nonetheless, and as the Full Court identified in Lenova & Lenova (Costs):
[A] limited financial capacity to meet an order cannot be determinative; if it were, a party would always be able to plead impecuniosity as a means of avoiding a cost order in circumstances where the pursuit of litigation has continued in the face of a reasonable offer to cease that litigation and the incurring of its attendant costs[186].
Further, and in Cross & Beaumont, the Full Court said as follows:
We do not suggest that the apparent inability of a party to pay costs is a bar to an order being made, since there are cases where the conduct of an impecunious party will warrant costs being ordered without regard to the difficulties likely to be associated with enforcement.[187]
For these reasons, I do not consider that the father’s financial circumstances preclude me from making a costs order in favour of the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
The father has been afforded a significant benefit in these proceedings from the assistance of the Independent Children’s Lawyer. This is a factor, which I bring to account pursuant to the terms of s 114UB(3)(g) of the Act.
It is the responsibility of the court to balance the various matters set out in section 114UB(3) of the Act to arrive at a result, which it considers just. I am conscious of the serious consequences for the father if an order for costs is made against him. That said, and having regard to the principles referred to by Kent J in Stoian & Flemming (Costs)[188], I am persuaded that it would be logical, fair and reasonable to require the father to meet the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer. However, and in circumstances where the father’s assets are modest, I will make an order requiring him to pay a sum of $7,000 only towards the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer such sum to be payable to Legal Aid New South Wales within ninety (90) days.

REFERRAL OF MR P TO THE LEGAL SERVICES COMMISSIONER

Mr P, solicitor, attended at Court on 16 September 2025 pursuant to the terms of a subpoena filed by the mother on 4 August 2025. The subpoena required at Part C for Mr P to attend at Court to give evidence. Mr P was the father’s former solicitor.
During the course of Mr P’s evidence, I learned that the father never executed a solicitor costs agreement nor did he provided written cost disclosure as required by s 174(1) of the Legal Profession Uniform Law (NSW). Mr P asserted that he was not obliged to make such disclosure in circumstances where on each occasion that he provided an account for professional services to the father, his fee did not exceed $750 (excluding Goods and Services Tax). The terms of s 174(4) of the Uniform Law are clear. That is, Mr P was only excluded from his disclosure obligations if his “total legal costs in the matter (excluding GST and disbursements) were likely to exceed” the sum of $750. A Costs Notice filed by the father’s solicitor on 24 October 2024 identified that by that juncture, the father’s fees and disbursements had been in the order of $6,150. Mr P failed to file Costs’ Notices as required by Rule 12.06 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 with respect to hearings, which were conducted on 21 November 2024 and 12 December 2024.
Mr P’s attendance on 16 September 2025 occurred against the background of an interim judgment, which I delivered on 7 February 2025, namely, Sanket & Zain [2025] FedCFamC1F 51. Orders 1 and 2 of the Orders made by me that day were in the following terms:
Without limiting his continuing obligation to make full disclosure in compliance with the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), by no later than 4.00 pm on 21 February 2025, the Applicant Husband (“the husband”) file and serve upon the Respondent Wife (“the wife”) a List of Documents disclosing:
Any Costs Agreement between the husband and his solicitor and/or any document, which:
Evidences the basis on which the husband’s solicitor would calculate his costs;
Evidences the precise work, which the husband’s solicitor was engaged to perform from time to time;
Communications or correspondence between the husband, and/or on his behalf, and the husband’s solicitor for the period from the date of the husband’s engagement of his solicitor to 4 December 2024 with respect to the parties’ competing financial applications;
All file notes created by the husband’s solicitor relevant to the parties’ competing financial applications for the period from the date of the husband’s engagement of his solicitor to 4 December 2024;
All records of telephone conversations between the husband, and/or on his behalf, and the husband’s solicitor and/or any member of his office with respect to the parties’ competing financial applications for the period from the date of the husband’s engagement of his solicitor to 4 December 2024;
Any document, which records in any manner whatsoever instructions provided by the husband to his solicitor with respect to the parties’ competing financial applications for the period from the date of the husband’s engagement of his solicitor to 24 October 2024;
All trust account statements, tax invoices and time recording records with respect to work performed by the husband’s solicitor or his office for the period from the date of the husband’s engagement of his solicitor to 4 December 2024;
Documents provided to the husband in which the husband’s solicitor and/or his office provided advice to the husband about his duty of disclosure with respect to financial issues;
All advice provided by the husband’s solicitor and/or his office to the husband in relation to the sale/transfer of the property located at [B Street], [Suburb C] in the State of New South Wales; and
All advice provided by the husband’s solicitor and/or his office to the husband in relation to the disposal of matrimonial assets either prior to or subsequent to the commencement of financial proceedings.
The list provided pursuant to Order 1 shall not include any document, which reveals:
Advice provided by the husband’s solicitor and/or his office to the husband with respect to any entitlement of the wife pursuant to her application under Part VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 AND to the extent that any document does reveal such advice, then the husband’s solicitor is permitted to redact the document;
Communications between the husband, and/or on his behalf, and the husband’s solicitor or any member of his office in relation to the parties’ competing parenting applications AND to the extent that any document does reveal such information, then the husband’s solicitor is permitted to redact the document.
The Orders of 7 February 2025 were made against the background of a significant dispute between the parties about the adequacy of disclosure made by the husband with respect to the wife’s application pursuant to s 106B of the Act and her application under Part VIII of the Act generally.
On cross-examination by the mother’s counsel, Mr P said that he did not have file notes in the “traditional sense” but may have “a few scratchings”. By way of his defence, Mr P said that he was often in his motor vehicle at the time that he took instructions from the father. However, and in circumstances where the father is not able to speak fluent English, instructions were often taken by telephone from the father’s friend, Mr N. Mr P’s evidence on 16 September 2025 was ultimately suspended by me in circumstances where it became apparent that Mr P had not brought his complete file to Court. At the time, I commented that it was difficult to comprehend how it might be that Mr P attended at Court knowing that he would be cross-examined about his retainer by the father and not bring his complete file to Court. His conduct resulted in unnecessary delay and a waste of time. As a consequence of his conduct, I made specific orders about the documents, which Mr P was obliged to bring to Court on the next occasion on which he gave evidence.
Mr P attended at Court again on 25 September 2025 and produced a large volume of material. Two aspects of that outcome cause me concern:
(a) The documents ought to have been produced pursuant to the orders made by me on 7 February 2025 in any event; and

(b) The documents ought to have been brought to Court by Mr P on 16 September 2025 when he attended to give evidence.

Mr P did neither of those things. This being so, and for the other reasons described above, I consider it is appropriate to refer Mr P to the Office of the New South Wales Legal Services Commissioner for investigation.
At the conclusion of the hearing, the mother’s counsel advised me that the mother wished to make an application for costs as a consequence of the conduct of Mr P described above. Accordingly, I will make an order that any application for costs by the mother be filed within twenty-eight (28) days of the delivery of this judgment.

REFERRAL OF MR O TO NSW FAIR TRADING AND THE PRESIDENT OF THE REAL ESTATE INSTITUTE OF NSW SOUTH WALES

Mr O of the J Real Estate Agency was the real estate agent engaged by the husband to sell the former matrimonial home.
On 22 March 2024, Mr O drafted an email in the following terms addressed to the husband:
Hi [Mr Sanket],

I have attached the agency agreement in this email and also the record of when the sale happened in September last year.

We did 1 open home which had roughly 10 buyers came through. However the feedback wasn’t that great due to the property being located in a heavy flood zone. Pretty much all buyers didn’t want to offer as theyre [sic] worried about then [sic] flood. The only person who made an offer was [the second respondent] which was $750,000 which we accepted as that was the best offer at that time.

My apologies for not being able to get you a higher price as the flood concerns and the interest rates have been causing alot [sic] of sale price [sic] to plumet [sic].
The email was admitted into evidence.[189]
On cross-examination by the wife’s counsel, Mr O confirmed that the content of the email was false and that there had been no negotiation with any third party whatsoever with respect to the sale of the property. Instead, he said that Mr N attended at his office in March 2024 (some five months after settlement) and asked him to prepare an email in the terms described above. Mr N is a person who has variously been described as:
(a) The husband’s friend; and

(b) The husband’s brother-in-law by dint of his marriage to the second respondent.

Mr N denied that allegation.

Whatever the scenario, Mr O prepared a document, which he has agreed was false. Schedule 1 to the Property and Stock Agents Regulation 2022 (NSW) identifies the general rules of conduct applying to all agents. Schedule 1, Regulation 3 is in the following terms:
Honest, fairness and professionalism
(1) An agent must act honestly, fairly and professionally with all parties in a transaction.

(2) An agent must not mislead or deceive a party in negotiations or a transaction.

By any measure, Mr O has not acted honestly by creating a document the terms of which were false. This being so, I have determined that it is in the public interest to refer Mr O to those bodies, which are responsible for the regulation of real estate agents in New South Wales.

CONCLUSION

The orders set out at the commencement of these reasons are those that most capably meet the child’s best interests.
